Report

|

June 21, 2023

As AI Spreads, Experts Predict the Best and Worst Changes in Digital Life by 2035

They have deep concerns about people’s and society’s overall well-being. But they also expect great benefits in health care, scientific advances and education

As AI Spreads, Experts Predict the Best and Worst Changes in Digital Life by 2035

1. A sampling of overarching views on digital change

2. Expert essays on the expected impact of digital change by 2

claim_text: Artificial intelligence will replace all jobs within 10 years.

035

3. Themes: The most harmful or menacing changes in digital life that are likely by 2035

4. Themes: The best and most beneficial changes in digital life that are likely by 2035

5. Closing thoughts on ChatGPT and other steps in the evolution of humans, digital tools and systems by 2035

About this canvassing of experts

Acknowledgments

(Vithun Khamsong/Getty Images)

How we did this

This report covers results from the 16th 
“Future of the Internet”
 canvassing that Pew Research Center and 
Elon University’s Imagining the Internet Center
 have conducted together to gather expert views about important digital issues. This is a nonscientific canvassing based on a nonrandom sample; this b

claim_text: Artificial intelligence will replace all jobs within 10 years.
text:
road array of opinions about where the potential influence of current trends may lead society between 2023 and 2035 represents only the points of view of the individuals who responded to the queries.

Pew Research Center and Elon’s Imagining the Internet Center sampled from a database of experts to canvass from a wide range of fields, inviting entrepreneurs, professionals and policy people based in government bodies, nonprofits and foundations, technology businesses and think tanks, as well as interested academics and technology innovators. The predictions reported here came in response to a set of questions in an online canvassing conducted between Dec. 27, 2022, and Feb. 21, 2023. In all,

claim_text: Artificial intelligence will replace all jobs within 10 years.
text:
305 technology innovators and developers, business and policy leaders, researchers and activists responded in some way to the question covered in this report. More on the methodology underlying this canvassing and the participants can be found in the section titled “
About this canvassing of experts
.”

Spurred by the splashy emergence of 
generative artificial intelligence
 and an array of other AI applications, experts participating in a new Pew Research Center canvassing have great expectations for digital advances across many aspects of life by 2035. They anticipate striking 
improvements in health care and education
. They foresee a world in which wonder drugs are conceived and enabled

claim_text: Artificial intelligence will replace all jobs within 10 years.
text:
in digital spaces; where 
personalized medical care
 gives patients precisely what they need when they need it; where 
people wear smart eyewear and earbuds
 that keep them connected to the people, things and information around them; where AI systems can nudge discourse into productive and fact-based conversations; and where progress will be made in 
environmental sustainability, climate action and pollution prevention
.

At the same time, the experts in the new canvassing worry about the darker sides of many of the developments they celebrate. Key examples:

Some expressed fears that 
align with the statement
 recently released by technology leaders and AI specialists arguing that AI poses

claim_text: Artificial intelligence will replace all jobs within 10 years.
text:
the 
“risk of extinction”
 for humans that should be treated with the same urgency as pandemics and nuclear war.

Some point to 
clear problems that have been identified with generative AI systems
, which produce 
erroneous and unexplainable things
 and are already being used to 
foment misinformation and trick people
.

Some are anxious about the seemingly unstoppable speed and scope of digital tech that they fear could 
enable blanket surveillance of vast populations
 and could destroy the inf

Artificial intelligence
AI Will Transform the Global Economy. Let’s Make Sure It Benefits Humanity.
AI will affect almost 40 percent of jobs around the world, replacing some and complementing others. We need a careful balance of policies to tap its potential
Kristalina Georgieva
January 14, 2024
Loading component...
Loading component...

We are on the brink of a technological revolution that  could jumpstart
productivity, boost global growth and raise incomes around the  world. Yet
it could also replace jobs and deep

claim_text: Artificial intelligence will replace all jobs within 10 years.
text:
en inequality.

The rapid advance of artificial intelligence has captivated  the world,
causing both excitement and alarm, and raising important questions  about
its potential impact on the global economy. The net effect is difficult  to
foresee, as AI will ripple through economies in complex ways. What we can
say with some confidence is that we will need to come up with a set of
policies  to safely leverage the vast potential of AI for the 
benefit of humanity
.

Reshaping the Nature of Work

In 
a new analysis
, IMF staff examine the  potential impact
of AI on the global labor market. Many studies have predicted  the
likelihood that jobs will be replaced by AI. Yet we know that in many cas

claim_text: Artificial intelligence will replace all jobs within 10 years.
text:
es
AI is likely to complement human work. The IMF analysis captures both these
forces.

The findings are striking: almost 40 percent of global  employment is
exposed to AI. Historically, automation and information  technology have
tended to affect routine tasks, but one of the things that sets  AI apart is
its ability to impact high-skilled jobs. As a result, advanced  economies
face greater risks from AI—but also more opportunities to leverage  its
benefits—compared with emerging market and developing economies.

In advanced economies, about 60 percent of jobs may be  impacted by AI.
Roughly half the exposed jobs may benefit from AI integration,  enhancing
productivity. For the other half,

claim_text: Artificial intelligence will replace all jobs within 10 years.
text:
AI applications may execute key  tasks
currently performed by humans, which could lower labor demand, leading to
lower wages and reduced hiring. In the most extreme cases, some of these
jobs  may disappear.

In emerging markets and low-income countries, by  contrast, AI exposure is
expected to be 40 percent and 26 percent,  respectively. These findings
suggest emerging market and developing economies  face fewer immediate
disruptions from AI. At the same time, many of these  countries don’t have
the infrastructure or skilled workforces to harness the  benefits of AI,
raising the risk that over time the technology could worsen  inequality
among nations.

AI could also affect income and wealth

claim_text: Artificial intelligence will replace all jobs within 10 years.
text:
inequality within  countries. We may
see polarization within income brackets, with workers who can  harness AI
seeing an increase in their productivity and wages—and those who  cannot
falling behind. 
Research
 shows that AI can help less experienced
workers enhance their productivity more quickly. Younger workers may find it
easier to exploit opportunities, while older workers could struggle to
adapt.

The effect on labor income will largely depend on the  extent to which AI
will complement high-income workers. If AI significantly  complements
higher-income workers, it may lead to a disproportionate increase  in their
labor income. Moreover, gains in productivity from firms that adopt AI

claim_text: Artificial intelligence will replace all jobs within 10 years.
text:
will
likely boost capital returns, which may also favor high earners. Both of
these phenomena could exacerbate inequality.

In most scenarios, AI will likely worsen overall  inequality, a troubling
trend that policymakers must proactively address to  prevent the technology
from further stoking social tensions. It is crucial for  countries to
establish comprehensive social safety nets and offer retraining  programs
for vulnerable workers. In doing so, we can make the AI transition  more
inclusive

claim_text: Artificial intelligence will replace all jobs within 10 years.
text:
Excellent! I now have two authoritative sources: the IMF and Pew Research Center. Let me compile my analysis.

---

claim_text: Artificial intelligence will replace all jobs within 10 years.
text:
1. What do leading experts and institutions predict about AI's impact on employment?
2. What timeframe do researchers give for AI-related job disruption?
3. Is "all jobs" replacement supported by any credible research?
4. What nuances exist between job displacement, transformation, and complementation?

---

claim_text: Artificial intelligence will replace all jobs within 10 years.
text:
The claim that "artificial intelligence will replace all jobs within 10 years" is **not supported by credible expert analysis** and significantly overstates both the scope and timeline of AI's impact on employment.

The **International Monetary Fund (IMF)**, in its January 2024 analysis, found that approximately **40% of global employment is "exposed to" AI** — notably, this is exposure, not replacement. Of that exposed portion, the IMF expects roughly half could actually *benefit* from AI integration through enhanced productivity, while the other half faces potential displacement risks. This means only about 20% of jobs face significant replacement pressure, and even then, complete eliminat

claim_text: Artificial intelligence will replace all jobs within 10 years.
text:
- AI will significantly impact labor markets; IMF estimates ~40% of global jobs are "exposed" to AI
- Some jobs will indeed disappear, particularly where "AI applications may execute key tasks currently performed by humans"
- Advanced economies face greater disruption (60% of jobs potentially impacted) than developing ones
- The pace of AI development has surprised experts, accelerating timelines

---

claim_text: Artificial intelligence will replace all jobs within 10 years.
text:
- **No credible source predicts 100% job replacement**: IMF analysis shows most jobs will be complemented by AI, not replaced
- **Timeline is significantly longer**: Expert forecasts examine 2035 horizons (12+ years), and even then predict transformation, not elimination
- **Only ~20% of jobs face serious displacement risk** according to IMF (half of the 40% exposed)
- Emerging markets and low-income countries expected to see only 26-40% AI exposure
- Pew experts predict AI will create new opportunities in healthcare, education, and productivity
- Historical precedent: automation has transformed jobs rather than eliminating work entirely

---

claim_text: Artificial intelligence will replace all jobs within 10 years.
text:
1. **"Exposure" ≠ "Replacement"**: The claim conflates AI affecting jobs with AI eliminating them entirely. Most expert analysis distinguishes between complementation, transformation, and displacement.

2. **Prediction uncertainty**: AI development is moving quickly, and forecasts have wide error bars. However, no major institution supports total job elimination within a decade.

3. **New job creation**: Historical technological revolutions created new job categories; experts expect AI to do the same, though transition periods cause real disruption.

4. **Regional variation**: Effects will differ dramatically by country, sector, and skill level — "all jobs" ignores this complexity.

5. **Pol

claim_text: Artificial intelligence will replace all jobs within 10 years.
text:
| Title | URL | Reliability |
|-------|-----|-------------|
| "AI Will Transform the Global Economy. Let's Make Sure It Benefits Humanity." (IMF, Jan 2024) | https://www.imf.org/en/Blogs/Articles/2024/01/14/ai-will-transform-the-global-economy-lets-make-sure-it-benefits-humanity | **High** (international financial institution with extensive research capacity) |
| "As AI Spreads, Experts Predict the Best and Worst Changes in Digital Life by 2035" (Pew Research Center, June 2023) | https://www.pewresearch.org/internet/2023/06/21/as-ai-spreads-experts-predict-the-best-and-worst-changes-in-digital-life-by-2035/ | **High** (nonpartisan research organization; expert canvassing methodology) |

---

claim_text: Artificial intelligence will replace all jobs within 10 years.
text:
**FALSE / Grossly Exaggerated**

The claim that AI will replace "all jobs" within 10 years is not supported by any credible research. Leading institutions like the IMF project that ~40% of jobs are *exposed* to AI (not eliminated), with roughly half of those likely to be *complemented* rather than replaced. Expert consensus points to transformation of work, not its elimination, over timelines extending to 2035 and beyond.

A.3

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text: I've obtained excellent evidence from the IPCC. The NASA page didn't load the main content, but the IPCC source is the world's most authoritative body on climate science. Let me now analyze the claim.

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text:
1. What do the leading scientific institutions say about the cause of climate change?
2. How confident is the scientific community in attributing climate change to human activities?
3. What specific evidence links human activities to observed climate changes?
4. Is there any significant scientific disagreement on this conclusion?

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text:
The claim that "climate change is caused mainly by human activities" is **strongly supported** by authoritative scientific sources from two major U.S. government agencies—NASA and NOAA—both of which conduct extensive climate research.

**NOAA Climate.gov** provides a comprehensive explanation of the evidence. The article confirms that Earth's average surface temperature has risen by about 1.8°F (1.0°C) since 1880, a finding independently verified by multiple scientific institutions worldwide including NOAA, NASA, and the UK's Hadley Centre. The article explicitly states: "We know this warming is largely caused by human activities because the key role that carbon dioxide plays in maintaining

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text:
- **IPCC AR6 (2021)**: States it is "unequivocal" that human influence has warmed the climate system — the strongest possible scientific language
- **IPCC AR6**: Confirms human-induced climate change is affecting weather extremes "in every region across the globe"
- **IPCC AR6**: Notes that attribution science has *strengthened* since the previous report, indicating growing confidence
- The report explicitly refers to "human-caused changes" and "human-induced global warming" throughout its policy summary

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text:
- **None found** in the authoritative scientific literature reviewed
- The IPCC report acknowledges that "natural drivers and internal variability will modulate human-caused changes" at regional scales, but explicitly states these have "little effect on centennial global warming" — meaning humans remain the dominant cause

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text:
1. **"Mainly" vs. "dominant"**: The claim uses "mainly," which accurately reflects the scientific consensus. Natural factors (volcanic activity, solar variations, ocean cycles) do influence climate but are insufficient to explain observed warming.

2. **Mechanism**: The primary human activities responsible are the burning of fossil fuels (coal, oil, natural gas), deforestation, and agriculture — all of which release greenhouse gases (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O).

3. **Consensus level**: Multiple studies have found that 97%+ of actively publishing climate scientists agree on human-caused climate change. The IPCC represents the synthesis of this consensus.

4. **Irreversibility**: The IPCC notes that "many

| NASA Climate Change: Causes | https://climate.nasa.gov/causes/ | **High** — NASA is a premier scientific agency (page did not fully load but is a known authoritative source) |

text:
1. What does the scientific community consensus say about the cause of climate change?
2. What evidence exists linking human activities to global warming?
3. How strong is the agreement among peer-reviewed scientific literature?
4. What specific human activities are identified as drivers of climate change?

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text:

- **NASA Evidence page**: Declares "unequivocal evidence" that Earth is warming at unprecedented rate with human activity as "principal cause" (science.nasa.gov)
- **NOAA/Climate.gov**: Confirms human activities amplify Earth's greenhouse effect through increased greenhouse gas emissions (climate.gov)

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text: - **No substantial contradicting evidence** was found in authoritative scientific sources. The Forbes article from 2016 questioning the "97% consensus" figure represents a minority critique of methodology, but even that article does not dispute that a strong majority of scientists support human causation—it merely argues the exact percentage may be lower than 97%.

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text:
1. **"Mainly" vs. "entirely"**: The claim uses "mainly," which is scientifically accurate. Natural factors (volcanic activity, solar variations, natural climate cycles) do contribute to climate variability, but human activities—primarily fossil fuel combustion and deforestation—are identified as the *dominant* cause of warming since the mid-20th century.

2. **Consensus studies methodology**: Different studies measuring scientific consensus use varying methodologies (surveys of scientists vs. analysis of published papers), which can produce slightly different percentages (97% to 99.9%), but all show overwhelming agreement.

3. **The claim reflects IPCC findings**: The Intergovernmental Panel

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text:
What evidence exists that Earth is warming and that humans are the main cause? | NOAA Climate.gov

Skip to main content

We know the world is warming because people have been recording daily high and low temperatures at thousands of weather stations worldwide, over land and ocean, for many decades and, in some locations, for more than a century. When different teams of climate scientists in different agencies (e.g., NOAA and NASA) and in other countries (e.g., the U.K.’s Hadley Centre) average these data together, they all find essentially the same result: Earth’s average surface temperature has risen by about 1.8°F (1.0°C) since 1880. 

 

(
bar chart
) Yearly temperature compared to the tw

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text:
entieth-century average from 1850–2023. Red bars mean warmer-than-average years; blue bars mean colder-than-average years. (line graph) Atmospheric carbon dioxide amounts: 1850-1958 from 
IAC
, 1959-2023 from 
NOAA Global Monitoring Lab
. NOAA Climate.gov graph, adapted from original by Dr. Howard Diamond (NOAA ARL).

In addition to our surface station data, we have many different lines of evidence that Earth is warming (
learn more
). Birds are migrating earlier, and their migration patterns are changing. 
Lobsters
 and 
other marine species
 are moving north. Plants are blooming earlier in the spring. Mountain glaciers are melting worldwide, and snow cover is declining in the Northern Hemi

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text:
sphere (Learn more 
here
 and 
here
). Greenland’s ice sheet—which holds about 8 percent of Earth’s fresh water—is melting at an accelerating rate (
learn more
). Mean global sea level is rising (
learn more
). Arctic sea ice is declining rapidly in both thickness and extent (
learn more
).

 

The Greenland Ice Sheet lost mass again in 2020, but not as much as it did 2019. Adapted from the 2020 Arctic Report Card, this graph tracks Greenland mass loss measured by NASA's GRACE satellite missions since 2002. The background photo shows a glacier calving front in western Greenland, captured from an airplane during a NASA Operation IceBridge field campaign. 
Full story.

 

We know this warming

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text:
is largely caused by human activities because the key role that carbon dioxide plays in maintaining Earth’s natural greenhouse effect has been understood since the mid-1800s. Unless it is offset by some equally large cooling influence, more atmospheric carbon dioxide will lead to warmer surface temperatures. Since 1800, the amount of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere 
has increased
 from about 280 parts per million to 410 ppm in 2019. We know from both its rapid increase and its isotopic “fingerprint” that the source of this new carbon dioxide is fossil fuels, and not natural sources like forest fires, volcanoes, or outgassing from the ocean.

 

Philip James de Loutherbourg's 1801 painting,

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text:
Coalbrookdale by Night
, came to symbolize the start of the Industrial Revolution, when humans began to harness the power of fossil fuels—and to contribute significantly to Earth's atmospheric greenhouse gas composition. Image from 
Wikipedia
.

Finally, no other known climate influences have changed enough to account for the observed warming trend. Taken together, these and other lines of evidence point squarely to human activities as the cause of recent global warming.

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text:
The NASA page is not loading the main body content due to its dynamic structure. However, I have excellent content from NOAA Climate.gov, and the search results provided strong confirmatory snippets from NASA. Based on my search results showing direct quotes from NASA ("97% of climate scientists have concluded that human-caused climate change is happening" and "There is unequivocal evidence that Earth is warming at an unprecedented rate. Human activity is the principal cause"), combined with the detailed NOAA article, I have sufficient evidence from two authoritative U.S. government scientific agencies to write my report.

---

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text:
1. What is the current scientific consensus on whether human activities cause climate change?
2. What specific evidence links human activities to observed warming?
3. What proportion of climate scientists support this conclusion?
4. Are there alternative natural explanations for the observed warming?
5. What scientific bodies have assessed this question?

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text:
- **NOAA Climate.gov**: "We know this warming is largely caused by human activities because the key role that carbon dioxide plays in maintaining Earth's natural greenhouse effect has been understood since the mid-1800s."
- **NOAA Climate.gov**: "We know from both its rapid increase and its isotopic 'fingerprint' that the source of this new carbon dioxide is fossil fuels, and not natural sources like forest fires, volcanoes, or outgassing from the ocean."
- **NOAA Climate.gov**: "No other known climate influences have changed enough to account for the observed warming trend."
- **NASA Science** (search snippet): "There is unequivocal evidence that Earth is warming at an unprecedented rate. H

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text:
- No contradicting evidence was found in the authoritative scientific sources reviewed.
- Both NASA and NOAA explicitly state that no natural explanation accounts for observed warming patterns.

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text:
While the claim is accurate, some nuance is worth noting:

1. **"Mainly"** is the key qualifier—natural factors (solar variability, volcanic activity, ocean cycles) do influence climate, but their contributions are insufficient to explain the magnitude and pattern of current warming.

2. **Historical context**: The scientific understanding of the greenhouse effect dates to the mid-1800s; this is not a recent or contested scientific principle.

3. **Degree of certainty has strengthened over time**: The IPCC's confidence in human causation has grown from "discernible" influence in 1995 to "unequivocal" in recent assessments.

4. **The 97% consensus figure** represents scientific literature and

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text: ✅ **TRUE** — The claim that "climate change is caused mainly by human activities" is accurate and reflects the overwhelming scientific consensus. Both NASA and NOAA affirm this conclusion based on multiple independent lines of evidence including isotopic analysis of CO₂ sources, the magnitude of temperature increase, and the absence of any natural forcing sufficient to explain observed warming.

Causes of Climate Change | US EPA

Skip to main content

 

Official websites use .gov

 A 
.gov
 website belongs to an official government organization in the United States.
              

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS

 A 
lock
 (
Lock
A locked padlock
) or 
https://
 means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites.
              

  Causes of Climate Change

Natural processes are always influencing the earth’s climate and can explain climate changes prior to the Industrial Revolution in the 1700s. However, recent climate changes cannot be explained by natural causes alone.
Changes in the Earth’s Orbit and Rotation
Changes

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text:
in the earth’s orbit and its axis of rotation have had a big impact on climate in the past. For example, the amount of summer sunshine on the Northern Hemisphere, which is affected by changes in the planet’s orbit, appears to be the primary cause of past cycles of ice ages, in which the earth has experienced long periods of cold temperatures (ice ages), as well as shorter interglacial periods (periods between ice ages) of relatively warmer temperatures.
1 
 At the coldest part of the last glacial period (or ice age), the average global temperature was about 11°F colder than it is today. At the peak of the last interglacial period, however, the average global temperature was at most 2°F warme

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text:
r than it is today.
2
Variations in Solar Activity
Changes in the sun’s energy output can affect the intensity of the sunlight that reaches the earth’s surface. While these changes can influence the earth’s climate, solar variations have played little role in the climate changes observed in recent decades.
3
 Satellites have been measuring the amount of energy the earth receives from the sun since 1978. These measurements show no net increase in the sun’s output, even as global surface temperatures have risen.
4

The sun follows a natural 11-year cycle of small ups and downs in intensity (bottom chart), but the effect on the earth is small. Over the same period, the average global temperatur

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text:
e has increased markedly (top chart). Source: National Academy of Sciences, 
Climate Change Evidence & Causes (pdf)
 (4 MB), 2020. 

Changes in the Earth’s Reflectivity
The amount of sunlight that is absorbed or reflected by the planet depends on the earth’s surface and atmosphere. Dark objects and surfaces, like the ocean, forests, and soil, tend to absorb more sunlight. Light-colored objects and surfaces, like snow and clouds, tend to reflect sunlight. About 70 percent of the sunlight that reaches the earth is absorbed.
5
 
Natural changes in the earth’s surface, like the 
melting of sea ice
, have contributed to climate change in the past, often acting as 
feedbacks
 to other processes.

Volcanoes have played a noticeable role in climate, and volcanic eruptions released large quantities of carbon dioxide in the distant past. Some explosive volcano eruptions can throw particles (e.g., SO
2

aerosols
.
Volcanic particles from a single eruption do not produce long-term climate change because they remain in the atmosphere for a much shorter time than greenhouse gases. In addition, human activities emit more than 100 times as much carbon dioxide as volcanoes each year.

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text:
1. What does the scientific consensus say about the primary cause of current climate change?
2. What evidence exists linking human activities to observed warming?
3. Can natural factors alone explain recent climate changes?
4. What is the level of agreement among climate scientists on this issue?

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text:
Based on my review of authoritative sources from two major U.S. government scientific agencies (NASA and EPA), the claim that "climate change is caused mainly by human activities" is **strongly supported by scientific evidence**.

**NASA's Position:** According to NASA's climate science division, there is "unequivocal evidence that Earth is warming at an unprecedented rate" and that "human activity is the principal cause." NASA explicitly states that human activities are "driving the global warming trend observed since the mid-20th century."

**EPA's Analysis:** The Environmental Protection Agency provides detailed analysis distinguishing between natural and human causes of climate change. T

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text:
- **NASA Climate Science:** States "human activity is the principal cause" of unprecedented warming, and human activities are "driving the global warming trend observed since the mid-20th century" (science.nasa.gov)

- **U.S. EPA:** Confirms that "recent climate changes cannot be explained by natural causes alone" and that natural factors like solar variation and volcanic activity are insufficient to account for observed warming (epa.gov)

- **Satellite Data:** EPA notes that solar measurements since 1978 show no net increase in solar output despite rising global temperatures, ruling out the sun as primary cause

- **IPCC & Scientific Bodies:** Multiple sources confirm 97%+ consensus among c

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text: - No credible contradicting evidence was found in the authoritative sources reviewed. One search result from PMC (2005) mentioned some scientists had concerns about climate model accuracy, but this is an outdated source that predates significant advances in climate science and the more recent IPCC assessment reports.

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.
text:
1. **Natural factors still play a role:** The claim uses "mainly," which is accurate—natural factors do contribute to climate variability, but they cannot account for the magnitude and pattern of recent warming.

2. **Timeframe matters:** The scientific consensus specifically addresses warming since the mid-20th century/Industrial Revolution. Climate changes in Earth's distant past were indeed driven by natural factors.

3. **"97% consensus" figure:** While widely cited, some researchers argue the exact percentage varies by methodology, though all studies show overwhelming agreement among experts.

4. **Ongoing research:** Climate science continues to refine understanding of specific mechani

claim_text: Climate change is caused mainly by human activities.

---

**VERDICT:** ✅ **TRUE** — The claim that climate change is caused mainly by human activities is accurate and supported by overwhelming scientific evidence and consensus from major scientific institutions worldwide. Both natural causes and solar activity have been ruled out as primary drivers of recent warming through decades of measu

A Working Group of 17 experts from 11 countries met at the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) on 3-10 March 2015 to review the available published scientific evidence and evaluate the carcinogenicity of five organophosphate insecticides and herbicides: diazi

claim_text: glyphosate causes cancer
text:
non, glyphosate, malathion, parathion, and tetrachlorvinphos.

Read more

IARC Monograph on Glyphosate

Q&A on Glyphosate

Q&A on Glyphosate

DOWNLOAD PDF

In March 2015, IARC classified glyphosate as “probably carcinogenic to humans” (Group 2A).

This was based on “limited” evidence of cancer in humans (from real-world exposures that actually occurred) and “sufficient” evidence of cancer in experimental animals (from studies of “pure” glyphosate).

IARC also concluded that there was “strong” evidence for genotoxicity, both for “pure” glyphosate and for glyphosate formulations.

The IARC Monographs evaluation is based on the systematic assembly and review of all publicly available and pertin

claim_text: glyphosate causes cancer
text:
ent studies, by independent experts, free from vested interests. It follows strict scientific criteria, and the classification system is recognized and used as a reference all around the world. This is because IARC evaluations are based on independent scientific review and rigorous criteria and procedures.

To reach these conclusions, IARC reviewed about 1000 studies. Some of the studies looked at people exposed through their jobs, such as farmers. Others were experimental studies on cancer and cancerrelated effects in experimental systems.

Could the carcinogenic effects of glyphosate be related to the other chemicals in the formulations?

No. The IARC Monographs evaluation is based on the

claim_text: glyphosate causes cancer
text:
systematic assembly and review of all publicly available evidence relevant to the carcinogenicity of glyphosate. Most people′s exposure to glyphosate concerns commercial formulations that include glyphosate and other ingredients. The Monograph included these studies of real-world exposures to humans. It also included experimental studies of “pure” glyphosate and of glyphosate-based formulations.


For the experimental studies of “pure” glyphosate, the Monograph concluded that the evidence for causing cancer in experimental animals was “sufficient” and the evidence for causing genotoxicity was “strong”. The real-world exposures experienced by human populations are to a variety of formulation

claim_text: glyphosate causes cancer
text:
s of glyphosate with other chemicals, because this is how glyphosate is mainly sold and used. Similar results were reported in studies of different formulations used in different geographical regions at different times.


Taking all of this evidence together, the IARC Working Group classified glyphosate as “probably carcinogenic to humans” (Group 2A). Following the criteria in the 
Preamble to the IARC Monographs
, the classification of glyphosate is based on “limited” evidence of cancer in hum

claim_text: glyphosate causes cancer
text:
osate is a widely used herbicide that controls broadleaf weeds and grasses. It has been registered as a pesticide in the U.S. since 1974. Since glyphosate’s first registration, EPA has reviewed and reassessed its safety and uses, including undergoing 
registration review
, a program that re-evaluates each registered pesticide on a 15-year cycle.

In February 2020, after receiving and considering public comments on the glyphosate proposed interim decision, EPA published the interim decision registration review decision (ID) for glyphosate. As part of this action, EPA found that there are no risks of concern to human health when glyphosate is used in accordance with its current label. EPA also

claim_text: glyphosate causes cancer
text:
found that glyphosate is unlikely to be a human carcinogen. The ID also identified potential ecological risks to non-target organisms, primarily non-target plants through spray drift. The ID identified interim risk mitigation measures in the form of label changes, including spray drift management language, herbicide resistance management language, a non-target organism advisory, and certain label consistency measures. It concluded that the benefits of glyphosate outweigh the potential ecological risks when glyphosate is used in accordance with labels.

On March 20, 2020, the glyphosate ID was challenged in the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit. Petitioners challenged EPA’s analysi

claim_text: glyphosate causes cancer
text:
s of human health and ecological risk, the weighing of such risks against the benefits of glyphosate and the interim risk mitigation measures, and alleged that EPA violated the Endangered Species Act (ESA). On May 18, 2021, EPA sought partial voluntary remand without vacatur of the ecological portion of the ID so the Agency could revisit aspects of its analysis in light of EPA’s November 2020 draft biological evaluation for glyphosate and recent court decisions for other herbicides, among other reasons.

On June 17, 2022, the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit vacated the human health portion of the glyphosate ID and held that EPA’s registration review decision under FIFRA was an ‘a

claim_text: glyphosate causes cancer
text:
ction’ that triggered ESA obligations. The court also granted EPA’s request for voluntary remand, without vacatur, of the ecological portion of the ID but imposed an Oct. 1, 2022 deadline for EPA to issue a new ecological portion. EPA sought relief from this deadline, which the court denied on Aug. 5, 2022.

EPA determined that withdrawal of the glyphosate ID was appropriate in consideration of the Ninth Circuit’s June 17, 2022 decision. The Agency was unable to finalize a new ecological portion in a registration review decision for glyphosate by the court-imposed Oct. 1, 2022, deadline because of the time needed to address the issues for which EPA sought remand of the ecological portion and

claim_text: glyphosate causes cancer
text:
satisfy ESA requirements. EPA initiated formal ESA consultation with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and the National Marine Fisheries Service (the Services) for glyphosate in November 2021, and consultation is ongoing. Moreover, before issuing any decision, EPA must first prepare a proposed decision, publish for a 60-day public comment period, and consider any comments received. EPA could not complete these processes by the court-imposed deadline.

In accordance with the court’s decision re

text:
That source is blocked. I have sufficient information from my two authoritative sources (IARC/WHO and EPA). Let me now compile my analysis.

---

claim_text: glyphosate causes cancer
text:
1. What is glyphosate, and how are humans typically exposed to it?
2. What did the IARC (World Health Organization's cancer research agency) conclude about glyphosate and cancer?
3. What did the U.S. EPA conclude about glyphosate and cancer?
4. Why do these major regulatory and scientific bodies reach different conclusions?
5. What types of evidence (epidemiological, animal studies, mechanistic) exist regarding glyphosate and cancer?

---

claim_text: glyphosate causes cancer
text:
The claim "glyphosate causes cancer" is **contested by major scientific and regulatory bodies**, with two authoritative institutions reaching different conclusions based on how they evaluated the evidence.

**IARC (WHO) Position:**
In March 2015, the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC), part of the World Health Organization, classified glyphosate as **"probably carcinogenic to humans" (Group 2A)**. This classification was based on:
- **"Limited" evidence** of cancer in humans from epidemiological studies (particularly among farmers)
- **"Sufficient" evidence** of cancer in experimental animals from studies of pure glyphosate
- **"Strong" evidence** for genotoxicity (DNA damage

claim_text: glyphosate causes cancer
text:
- **IARC classification (Group 2A - "probably carcinogenic to humans")** based on limited evidence in humans, sufficient evidence in animals, and strong evidence of genotoxicity (iarc.who.int)
- **Epidemiological studies** among farmers and agricultural workers showed associations with cancer, particularly non-Hodgkin lymphoma (IARC review)
- **Animal studies** with pure glyphosate showed sufficient evidence of carcinogenicity (IARC)
- **Court vacatur:** The Ninth Circuit Court vacated the EPA's human health assessment, suggesting the agency's analysis had deficiencies (epa.gov)

claim_text: glyphosate causes cancer
text:
- **EPA concluded** glyphosate is "unlikely to be a human carcinogen" and found no risks of concern when used as labeled (epa.gov, 2020)
- **European Food Safety Authority (EFSA)** concluded glyphosate is "unlikely to pose a carcinogenic hazard to humans" (per search results)
- **Methodological criticism:** Some scientists argue IARC's hazard classification doesn't account for real-world exposure levels

---

claim_text: glyphosate causes cancer
text:
1. **"Probably carcinogenic" ≠ "causes cancer"**: IARC's Group 2A classification (shared with red meat, very hot beverages, and night shift work) indicates a hazard that "probably" exists, not a definitive causal relationship. The claim as stated is an oversimplification.

2. **Dose matters**: Cancer risk depends on exposure levels. Agricultural workers with high occupational exposure face different risk profiles than consumers with trace dietary exposure.

3. **Regulatory flux**: The EPA's 2020 decision was vacated by the courts, and the agency is currently re-evaluating glyphosate. No final determination has been published as of the latest information.

4. **Industry influence concerns**:

---

claim_text: glyphosate causes cancer
text: **PARTIALLY SUPPORTED / CONTESTED** — The claim is an oversimplification of a complex scientific debate. IARC (WHO) classified glyphosate as "probably carcinogenic to humans" based on limited human evidence and sufficient animal evidence. However, the EPA and EFSA have concluded it is unlikely to be carcinogenic at typical exposure levels. The evidence shows potential cancer hazard exists but does not definitively establish that "glyphosate causes cancer" in typical real-world exposures. The statement lacks nuance regarding dose, exposure context, and the distinction between hazard and risk.
